evidence_excerpt: At age 56 years she had progressive, bilateral visual field loss and markedly reduced dynamic visual acuity with low horizontal VOR gain; neuroimaging earlier showed cerebellar atrophy and basal ganglia calcifications, suggesting progressive neurodegeneration.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Paracentral visual loss affected reading efficiency and dynamic visual acuity fell to worse than J15 with head oscillations; bilateral vestibular hypofunction caused oscillopsia with head movement.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: She had a cochlear implant and was able to hear and function well.
Post-implant she was able to hear and function well.
An episode of rhabdomyolysis was triggered by the use of statins to lower cholesterol.
At age 50, automated perimetry showed bilateral visual field loss with elevated foveal thresholds.
At age 56, there was progression of field loss bilaterally and dynamic visual acuity dropped to worse than J15 with 2 Hz head oscillations.
At age 56, mean horizontal VOR gain was significantly low bilaterally with overt corrective saccades, consistent with bilateral vestibular loss.

notes_on_uncertainty: The record describes two patients with MIDD. This summary focuses on Patient 2 because longitudinal follow-up (ages 36, 50, and 56) provides clearer post-diagnosis events. Exact dates are not provided; timing is approximated relative to the diagnosis around age 36 when the mutation was identified and treatment (cochlear implant) was planned.
